1: Elisha’s oracle begins with šimʿû dĕbar-YHWH — ‘hear the word of YHWH’ — the standard call to prophetic attention. The prediction: kĕsāʾāh sōlet bĕšeqel ûsātayim śĕʿōrîm bĕšeqel — ‘a seah of fine flour for a shekel, two seahs of barley for a shekel.’ This is a dramatic price reversal from 6:25 (where a donkey’s head costs 80 shekels). The timing: kāʿēt māḥār, ‘about this time tomorrow.’ The preciseness of the timing is part of the prophecy’s verification function.
2: The officer’s objection: wĕhinnēh ʾĕlōhîm ʿōśeh ʾărubbôt baššāmāyim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — ‘even if YHWH were to make windows in heaven, could this thing happen?’ The phrase ʾărubbôt baššāmāyim (windows in heaven) is taken directly from Gen 7:11 — the flood narrative where ‘the windows of heaven were opened.’ The officer’s sarcasm uses the flood idiom to mock the possibility of supernatural provision. Elisha’s response: hinnĕkā rōʾeh bĕʿênêkā ûmišām lōʾ tōʾkēl, ‘you shall see it with your own eyes but not eat of it.’
3: The lepers at the gate: ʾarbaʿāh ʾănāšîm hāyû mĕṣōrāʿîm — ‘four men with skin disease.’ The word mĕṣōrāʿîm (from ṣāraʿ) denotes a severe skin condition requiring social exclusion (Lev 13-14); their position at the gate entrance places them at the margin between the city and the outside — they are excluded from the community. Their logic is expressed in a binary: they can neither enter the city (famine within) nor stay at the gate (starvation there too).
4: The lepers’ reasoning uses the imperfect tense for both contingencies: ʾim-ʾāmarnû nāḇôʾ hāʿîr wĕhārāʿāb bāʿîr ûmātnû šām — ‘If we say, let us enter the city, the famine is in the city and we will die there.’ The structure is a classic Hebrew conditional: ʾim + imperfect... wĕ-perfect. The conclusion: wĕnābôʾ ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām ʾim-yĕḥayûnû niḥyeh, ‘if they spare us alive, we shall live; if they kill us, we shall die.’ This double conditional (ʾim-yĕḥayûnû / ʾim-yĕmîtûnû) is the syntax of a calculated gamble on mercy.
5: wayyāqûmû bannešep lābōʾ ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām — ‘they rose at twilight to go to the Aramean camp.’ Nešep means twilight, the ambiguous light between day and night. The temporal detail heightens suspense. They arrive at the edge of the camp (qĕṣēh maḥănēh ʾărām) — and wĕhinnēh ʾên-šām ʾîš, ‘and behold, there was no one there.’ The hinnēh particle introduces a dramatic reversal discovery.
6: The divine action: waʾdōnāy hišmîaʿ ʾet-maḥănēh ʾărām qôl rekeb — ‘the Lord had made the Aramean camp hear the sound of chariots.’ The verb hišmîaʿ (Hiphil of šāmaʿ, to hear) means to cause to hear — God made them hear a sound that was not physically present. The sounds catalogued: chariots (rekeb), horses (sûs), a great army (ḥayil gāḏôl). The Arameans interpreted it as a coalition of Egypt and the Hittite kings (Israel hired them, they said) — a false reading of a true divine signal.
7: The rout of the Arameans: wayyāqûmû wayyanûsû bannešep — ‘they rose and fled at twilight.’ The same word nešep as v5 — the lepers came at twilight; the Arameans fled at twilight. The temporal irony binds the two groups: the marginalized move toward hope while the powerful move in terror. They abandoned their camp fully equipped: ʾohŏlêhem wĕsûsêhem waḥămōrêhem — tents, horses, donkeys — and wayyibrĕḥû ʾel-napšām, ‘they fled for their lives.’ Nepeš (life, soul) here means their physical survival.
8: The lepers’ discovery: wayyābōʾû hamĕṣōrāʿîm hāʾēlleh ʾel-qĕṣēh hammahăneh — ‘these lepers came to the edge of the camp.’ They ‘came to’ the edge, ate, drank, carried off silver, gold, and clothing. The verb wayyiśśĕʾû (they carried off) followed by the accumulation of wealth: kesep wĕzāhāḇ ûbĕḡāḏîm. Their immediate response is private hoarding — they carry it and hide it (wayyiṭmĕnû, from ṭāman, to bury/conceal) before returning for more.
9: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾîš ʾel-rēʿēhû lōʾ-kēn ʾănaḥnû ʿōśîm — ‘They said to one another, We are not doing right.’ The word kēn (right, thus) in the construction lōʾ-kēn means ‘not rightly.’ The key phrase: yôm-bĕśôrāh hûʾ — ‘this is a day of good news.’ Bĕśôrāh is the Hebrew equivalent of euangelion — the announcement of something that changes everything. The same root (Piel of bāśar) is used in Isa 52:7 (‘how beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news’). The lepers recognize their obligation to proclaim, not hoard, the discovery.
10: The lepers call to the city gatekeepers — wayyiqrĕʾû ʾel-šōʿar hāʿîr — and report: bānû ʾel-maḥănēh ʾărām wĕhinnēh ʾên-šām ʾîš wĕqôl ʾāḏām, ‘we came to the Aramean camp and behold, there was no one there, and no sound of a human voice.’ The repeated hinnēh (behold) marks the surprise. The compound negative (ʾên-šām ʾîš + wĕqôl ʾāḏām) emphasizes total abandonment — both persons and sounds are absent.
11: The gatekeepers call out; the report reaches the palace: wayyuggad lĕbêt hammelek pĕnîmāh — ‘it was reported to the king’s house inside.’ The adverb pĕnîmāh (inside, inward) and the passive wayyuggad (it was told) shift the verb to the divine passive — news reaches the center of power by unseen agents.
12: The king rises at night — the same ironic timing as the Aramean flight (twilight/night). His suspicion: ʾaggîḏāh-nā lākem ʾēt ʾăšer-ʿāśû-lānû ʾărām — ‘let me tell you what the Arameans have done.’ He suspects a trap: yōdĕʿîm hēmmāh kî-rĕʿēbîm ʾănaḥnû, ‘they know we are hungry.’ His military logic is sound — but his reading of the situation is completely wrong. The narrative irony is that the king’s elaborate suspicion keeps him from acting on genuine deliverance.
13: The king’s servant suggests a modest reconnaissance: wĕyišlĕḥû wĕnirʾeh — ‘let us send and see.’ The argument: the horses that remain are ‘like all the multitude of Israel that will be destroyed’ (kĕkol-hāmôn yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer niš’ārû-ḇām). The syntax implies they are expendable — even if it’s a trap, the loss of five horses equals only the guaranteed loss of dying in the city. This is the logic of the lepers in v4, applied by the king’s servants: marginal losses are acceptable when total loss is the alternative.
14: Two chariot teams are sent (šĕnê rikbê sûsîm). The king commands: lĕkû ûrĕʾû — ‘go and see.’ The brief direct command uses the plural imperative without elaboration — the mission is simple. The king adds: wĕšallĕḥēm ʾaḥărê maḥănēh ʾărām, ‘send them after the Aramean camp.’ The verb šālaḥ (send forth) with ʾaḥărê (after) marks pursuit — the king thinks this might be a rearguard trap.
15: The scouts follow to the Jordan: wayyēlĕkû ʾaḥărêhem ʿaḏ-hayyardēn. The road was littered with garments and equipment the Arameans threw off in their panic — kol-hadderek mĕlēʾāh bĕḡāḏîm ûkēlîm. The word kēlîm (vessels, equipment) indicates military gear abandoned in flight. The scouts return and report. The abandoned equipment confirms the divine rout — the Arameans stripped themselves to flee faster, a sign of total panic.
16: wayyēṣēʾ hāʿām wayyābōzẑû ʾet-maḥănēh ʾărām — ‘the people went out and plundered the Aramean camp.’ The verb bāzaz (to plunder, spoil) is used of the Israelites plundering Egypt at the Exodus (Exod 12:36). The echo is intentional: YHWH’s deliverance of Israel from a siege produces the same plundering of the enemy’s goods. The price of flour immediately fulfills Elisha’s prophecy: kĕsāʾāh sōlet bĕšeqel ûsātayim śĕʿōrîm bĕšeqel, exactly as predicted.
17: wĕhammelek hipqîd ʾet-haššālîš ʾăšer niš’ān ʾal-yāḏô — ‘the king placed the officer on whose hand he had leaned in charge of the gate.’ Niš’ān ʾal-yāḏô (leaned on his hand) — the physical posture of royal support/dependence echoes the idiom of 2 Kgs 5:18 (Naaman leaning on his master’s arm). This officer is the one who mocked the prophecy (v2). The passive wayĕḇûsēhû — ‘the people trampled him’ — fulfills Elisha’s word: seeing but not eating.
18: The narrative repeats the prophecy verbatim — kĕdabbēr ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm ʾel-hammelek (as the man of God had spoken to the king). The repetition is the narrator’s verification: the prophetic word was given (v1-2), doubted (v2b), and is now confirmed (vv18-20). The structural feature — prediction, doubt, fulfillment, verification — is typical of the Kings narrator’s method of establishing prophetic credentials.
19: The doubting officer’s words are recalled: ʾapōt bĕšāmayim hăyihyeh haddāḇār hazzeh — ‘even if YHWH were to make windows in heaven, could this thing happen?’ The repetition serves as the legal basis for Elisha’s specific judgment (hinnĕkā rōʾeh bĕʿênêkā ûmiššām lōʾ tōʾkēl) — the man who disbelieved the word will see but not benefit. The word rāʾāh (see) connects to the eye-opening theme of chapter 6.
20: wayyĕhî-lô kēn — ‘and it happened exactly so to him.’ The terse narrator’s formula (kēn, thus/so) closes the Elisha prophecy narrative with economical precision: the officer was trampled in the gate and died. The waw-consecutive perfect wayyāmot (and he died) is the final word. The narrative circle: prophecy given → doubted → fulfilled → fulfilled exactly. Each link is verbally confirmed by the narrator.
